title: 怪屋
summary:
Sophia explains the internal dynamics of her "cold-blooded" family to Charles Haywood. She defends Brenda Leoni, describing her as a proper lady who was likely attracted to the charming and self-confident Aristide. Sophia characterizes her relatives by various forms of emotional detachment: her grandfather was a man who once stabbed two men in Smyrna without remorse; her grandmother was an arrogant woman who controlled matters of life and death; her mother is a self-centered actress; Clementine, Roger's wife, is a ruthless scientist; Roger's uncle is kind but temperamental; and Sophia's father is excessively reserved. Sophia expresses a strong desire to uncover the truth regarding Aristide's death.

As they walk toward the house, they are joined by Aunt Edith de Havilland, a woman in her seventies with a sharp gaze. Edith recognizes Charles and mentions his father. She expresses disdain for the police and discusses the reliability of old-fashioned maids. Reflecting on the strangeness of Aristide's poisoning, Edith notes that while she did not like him, his death leaves the house feeling empty. Edith reveals she has lived at the estate for forty years, having arrived after her sister Muriel married a "short, ugly foreigner." She explains that she stayed because of her love for the garden and because Philip Leonidis, a writer of obscure historical books, kept her there after his actress wife left him.
